title: Romans 5
subtitle: Peace with God Through Our Lord Jesus Christ

sections.header: Verses 1–11 — "We Rejoice in the Hope of the Glory of God"

heb.paragraph: Eirēnē in v. 1 denotes not merely a subjective feeling of tranquillity but an objective state of reconciliation. The enmity between God and sinners (cf. v. 10, ‘enemies’) has been ended through Christ’s atoning work. The term echoes the Hebrew shalom, which encompasses wholeness, well-being, and restored relationship. The textual variant between echomen (‘we have,’ indicative) and echōmen (‘let us have,’ subjunctive) is significant: most commentators favour the indicative, reading it as a declaration of fact rather than an exhortation.

Katallagē (vv. 10–11) is a distinctively Pauline term. Unlike hilastērion (propitiation), which focuses on satisfying God’s wrath, katallagē emphasises the restored relationship between God and humanity. Paul stresses that reconciliation was accomplished ‘while we were still enemies’ (v. 10) — the initiative was entirely God’s, grounded in love rather than human merit.

refs.note: Those who were far off have been brought near through the blood of Christ — reconciliation and peace through the cross.
God reconciled all things through Christ, making peace through his blood shed on the cross.
James presents a parallel chain: trials produce perseverance, and perseverance produces maturity.

notes.note: Peace with God is the first and foundational fruit of justification. It is not a feeling but a fact — the war between God and the sinner is over, the verdict is rendered, and hostility is ended.

Suffering is not purposeless but pedagogical. The chain suffering → perseverance → character → hope is not automatic but works through the indwelling Holy Spirit, who ‘pours out’ God’s love into our hearts (v. 5).
This is the definitive statement of God’s love: Christ died for us not when we were deserving but when we were sinners. The timing proves the love is unconditional.
An a fortiori argument: if God justified us by Christ’s death when we were enemies, how much more will he save us through Christ’s life now that we are reconciled. The greater secures the lesser.
Paul adds a third term to peace and hope: ‘we rejoice in God.’ Joy is the crowning fruit of reconciliation — not joy in our circumstances but joy in the God who saved us.

Calvin insists that the peace described here is objective, not subjective. It does not depend on the believer’s feelings but on Christ’s accomplished work. Even in seasons of spiritual distress, the justified person has peace with God.

Calvin sees this verse as the ultimate refutation of any merit-based soteriology. God’s love is directed toward sinners, not toward the deserving. If love awaited worthiness, no one would ever be loved.

The textual variant between echomen (indicative, ‘we have’) and echōmen (subjunctive, ‘let us have’) is one of the most discussed in the NT. The subjunctive has stronger manuscript support but the indicative makes better contextual sense, since Paul is declaring a fact, not issuing an exhortation.
The verb ekkechytai (‘has been poured out’) is a perfect tense, indicating a past action with continuing effects. God’s love is not a trickle but a flood.

Moo reads this section as a bridge paragraph that both concludes the justification argument (chs. 1–4) and introduces the sanctification/glorification argument (chs. 5–8). The key word is ‘hope’ (elpis) — the forward-looking dimension of the already-justified life.
Moo highlights the a fortiori logic as Paul’s primary assurance argument. The greater act (justification while enemies) guarantees the lesser (final salvation now that we are reconciled).

Schreiner emphasises that peace, access, and hope form a triad of justification’s benefits. The suffering chain (vv. 3–4) demonstrates that hope is not wishful thinking but is grounded in God’s demonstrated love (v. 5) and proven through the crucible of experience.

Schreiner argues that the four descriptions of humanity — ‘powerless’ (v. 6), ‘ungodly’ (v. 6), ‘sinners’ (v. 8), ‘enemies’ (v. 10) — are cumulative, not sequential. They describe the same condition from different angles, magnifying the grace of Christ’s death.

hist.context: Romans 5:1–11 transitions from the doctrinal exposition of justification (chs. 1–4) to its experiential consequences. The opening ‘therefore’ (oun) draws out the implications: peace, access to grace, hope, and reconciliation are the immediate fruits of justification. The passage also introduces the theme of suffering (thlipsis, v. 3), which Paul does not minimise but integrates into a chain of character formation: suffering → perseverance → character → hope. The climax is v. 8, often called ‘the greatest verse on the love of God’: ‘while we were still sinners, Christ died for us.’

sections.header: Verses 12–21 — "Adam and Christ: Death and Life"

heb.paragraph: Paul uses parapthma (trespass, a stepping aside) rather than hamartia (sin) in the Adam–Christ comparison, though both appear. The trespass is Adam’s specific act of disobedience in the garden, which has universal consequences. The distinction matters: Adam’s one parapthma brought condemnation to all (v. 18); Christ’s one act of righteousness (dikaiōma) brought justification to all.

Charisma appears three times in this passage (vv. 15, 16) and derives from charis (grace). Paul uses it to emphasise that the work of Christ is not merely the reversal of Adam’s sin but its superabundant overcoming. The gift is ‘not like’ the trespass (v. 15) because grace exceeds the damage of sin.

refs.note: The narrative of Adam’s disobedience — the foundational event behind Paul’s Adam–Christ typology.
Paul’s parallel Adam–Christ comparison: ‘as in Adam all die, so in Christ all will be made alive.’
The ‘last Adam’ / ‘second man’ Christology that complements the Romans 5 argument.

notes.note: Death entered the world through one man’s sin. The universal scope is absolute: ‘death came to all people, because all sinned.’ The verb ‘sinned’ (hēmarton) indicates participation in Adam’s transgression, not merely personal sinfulness.
The ‘how much more’ (pollō mallon) is Paul’s way of saying that grace is not merely equal to sin but superabundant. The gift does not merely cancel the trespass; it overwhelms it.

Those who receive the ‘abundance of grace’ will ‘reign in life.’ The passive recipients of grace become active rulers in the new creation. This is not merely restoration to Adam’s pre-fall state but elevation beyond it.

The obedience of Christ is both active (his life of perfect law-keeping) and passive (his submission to the cross). Together they constitute the ‘one act of righteousness’ (v. 18) that secures justification.

The law ‘entered so that the trespass might increase’ — not to cause sin but to expose its true magnitude. Where sin increased, grace ‘increased all the more’ (hypereperisseusen). Grace always outpaces sin.

Calvin reads ‘all sinned’ as participation in Adam’s guilt, not merely imitation of Adam’s example. Original sin is a hereditary corruption passed from Adam to all his descendants, rendering them guilty before God from birth.

Calvin emphasises the parallel structure: one trespass → condemnation for all; one act of righteousness → justification for all. The ‘all’ in the second clause is those who are in Christ by faith, not universally all humanity without exception.

The phrase eph hō (‘because’) has been interpreted as ‘in whom’ (all sinned in Adam, Augustine’s Latin reading), ‘because’ (all sinned individually), or ‘with the result that’ (all sinned as a consequence of Adam). The causal reading (‘because’) has the strongest grammatical support.
The term polloi (‘the many’) does not mean a subset but is a Semitism for ‘the great number’ / ‘the totality.’ Paul uses it interchangeably with ‘all’ in this passage.

Moo identifies this passage as the hinge between chapters 1–4 (justification) and chapters 6–8 (life in Christ). The Adam–Christ typology provides the theological foundation for union with Christ: just as all were ‘in Adam’ by natural solidarity, so believers are ‘in Christ’ by grace. The passage is not a digression but the necessary bridge.

Moo takes ‘because all sinned’ to mean that all people sinned in and through Adam’s transgression. Adam’s sin is not merely exemplary but constitutive: his disobedience actually brings condemnation to all his descendants.

Schreiner reads the passage through the lens of federal headship: Adam and Christ are representative figures whose actions determine the destiny of those they represent. The asymmetry is crucial: sin’s reign through Adam is real but finite; grace’s reign through Christ is both real and superabundant.

Schreiner argues that the ‘increase’ of sin through the law is a necessary stage in salvation history. The law must expose the full depth of sin so that grace can be seen as truly superabundant. The law is not the enemy but sin’s unwitting revealer.

hist.context: Romans 5:12–21 is Paul’s Adam–Christ typology, one of the most theologically consequential passages in the Bible. The argument compares two representative heads of humanity: Adam, through whose disobedience sin and death entered the world, and Christ, through whose obedience righteousness and life are given. The passage is the primary NT basis for the doctrines of original sin (Augustine) and federal headship (Reformed theology). Paul’s anacoluthon in v. 12 (the sentence begun with ‘just as’ is never completed with its expected ‘so also’ until v. 18) reveals the difficulty of stating the comparison concisely: at every turn, grace exceeds sin.

themes.note: Mercy peaks at 9: this chapter is saturated with grace language. Sovereignty at 8 reflects the Adam–Christ typology where God’s sovereign initiative in Christ overwhelms Adam’s ruin.

rows.text: Peace, Access, and Hope: The Fruits of Justification

rows.label: vv.6–11
rows.text: God’s Love Demonstrated: While We Were Still Sinners

rows.label: vv.12–14
rows.text: Death Through Adam: Sin Enters the World

rows.label: vv.15–21
rows.text: The Gift That Exceeds the Trespass

lit.note: Romans 5 is the bridge from justification (chs. 1–4) to sanctification (chs. 6–8). The first half celebrates justification’s fruits; the second half establishes the Adam–Christ framework that grounds everything that follows.

hebtext.note: Eirēnē in v. 1 denotes not merely a subjective feeling of tranquillity but an objective state of reconciliation. The enmity between God and sinners (cf. v. 10, ‘enemies’) has been ended through Christ’s atoning work. Katallagē (vv. 10–11) is a distinctively Pauline term. Unlike hilastērion (propitiation), which focuses on satisfying God’s wrath, katallagē emphasises the restored relationship between God and humanity. Paul stresses that reconciliation was accomplished ‘while we were still enemies’ (v. 10) — the initiative was entirely God’s, grounded in love rather than human merit.

The distinction matters: Adam’s one parapthma brought condemnation to all (v. 18); Christ’s one act of righteousness (dikaiōma) brought justification to all.

Charisma appears three times in this passage (vv. 15, 16) and derives from charis (grace). Paul uses it to emphasise that the work of Christ is not merely the reversal of Adam’s sin but its superabundant overcoming. The gift is ‘not like’ the trespass (v. 15) because grace exceeds the damage of sin.

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

translations.text: and endurance produces character, and character produces hope,
You see, at just the right time, when we were still powerless, Christ died for the ungodly.
For while we were still weak, at the right time Christ died for the ungodly.

Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads Peace with God Through Our Lord Jesus Christ within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.

discourse.thesis: Justified by faith, we have peace with God; and just as sin and death entered through Adam, grace and life abound through Christ.

nodes.text: Since we have been justified by faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ, through whom we have access to grace and rejoice in hope of glory.

More than that, we rejoice in sufferings — suffering produces perseverance, perseverance character, character hope. This hope does not disappoint because God's love has been poured into our hearts through the Holy Spirit.
While we were still weak, Christ died for the ungodly. Rarely will someone die for a righteous person — but God demonstrates his own love in this: while we were still sinners, Christ died for us.

If justified by his blood, how much more shall we be saved from wrath through him? If reconciled by his death, how much more shall we be saved by his life? We also rejoice in God through whom we have received reconciliation.

Sin entered the world through one man, and death through sin — death spread to all because all sinned. Sin was in the world before the law, yet death reigned from Adam to Moses, even over those whose sins were not like Adam's transgression. Adam is a type (τύπος) of the one to come.

The gift is not like the trespass. If many died through one man's trespass, much more have grace and the gift overflowed to many through one man, Jesus Christ. The gift is not like the result of one man's sin — judgment brought condemnation, but grace brought justification despite many trespasses.

children.text: If death reigned through one man's trespass, much more will those who receive grace reign in life through Christ Jesus.

nodes.text: As one trespass led to condemnation for all, so one act of righteousness leads to justification and life for all. As one man's disobedience made many sinners, so one man's obedience makes many righteous. Law increased trespass — but where sin increased, grace abounded all the more, so that as sin reigned in death, grace reigns through righteousness to eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord.

discourse.note: The Adam-Christ typology (5:12-21) is Paul's most developed statement on original sin and corporate solidarity. The five 'much more' (πολλῷ μᾶλλον) arguments amplify the superiority of grace over sin.

stories.title: Peace with God: Fact or Exhortation?
stories.summary: A single Greek letter changes the meaning entirely. With omicron (ἔχομεν, echomen), the verb is indicative: 'we have peace with God.' With omega (ἔχωμεν, echōmen), it is subjunctive: 'let us have peace with God.' The former states a fact about justification; the latter urges the reader to appropriate that peace.
stories.consensus: External evidence overwhelmingly favors the subjunctive, but transcriptional and contextual arguments favor the indicative. In rapid dictation, omicron and omega sounded identical. Paul's argument in Romans 5 states consequences of justification (facts), not exhortations. Most scholars accept the indicative as original despite the manuscript evidence.
stories.significance: This is one of the most famous cases where internal evidence overrides external. The indicative fits Paul's flow: since we are justified, we have peace. The subjunctive would be an odd exhortation in a passage stating the results of justification.

coaching.tip: 'We rejoice in our sufferings, knowing that suffering produces endurance' (v. 3). The chain: suffering → endurance → character → hope. Paul doesn't celebrate pain but its product. Hope isn't naive optimism; it's forged in furnaces. The logic assumes transformation: properly processed suffering doesn't embitter but matures.

'Just as sin came into the world through one man... so one man's act of righteousness leads to justification and life for all' (vv. 12, 18). The Adam-Christ parallel structures Paul's soteriology: solidarity in Adam (death), solidarity in Christ (life). One representative's act affects all represented. Headship is the key.
